docs.title: Workloads Overview
docs.text: This section contains production-style workload labs organized by domain.

docs.title: Available workload guides
docs.text:
• Time-Series Workloads
  • Vector Workloads
  • Full-Text Search Workloads
  • Geospatial Workloads
  • Document/JSON Workloads
  • BLOB Workloads
  • Graph Workloads
  • Memory Workloads
  • Governance Controls Workloads

docs.title: Lab assumptions
docs.text:
• MonkDB is running locally (recommended: 3-node compose)
  • You can connect with psql
  • Examples use doc schema unless stated otherwise

docs.title: Time-Series Workloads
docs.text: Time-series workloads in MonkDB typically combine high-ingest writes with aggregate-heavy reads.

docs.title: Create table
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS doc.sensor_data (
  ts TIMESTAMPTZ PRIMARY KEY,
  location TEXT NOT NULL,
  temperature REAL NOT NULL,
  humidity REAL NOT NULL,
  wind_speed REAL NOT NULL
)
CLUSTERED BY (ts) INTO 4 SHARDS
WITH (number_of_replicas = '0-1');

docs.title: Insert sample rows
docs.text:
INSERT INTO doc.sensor_data (ts, location, temperature, humidity, wind_speed) VALUES
  ('2026-03-14T10:00:00Z', 'New York', 20.2, 61.0, 12.4),
  ('2026-03-14T10:01:00Z', 'London',   14.6, 71.5,  8.3),
  ('2026-03-14T10:02:00Z', 'Berlin',   16.8, 65.2, 10.7),
  ('2026-03-14T10:03:00Z', 'Tokyo',    19.1, 59.3,  6.4);

REFRESH TABLE doc.sensor_data;

docs.title: Query patterns
docs.text:
Recent window:

SELECT ts, location, temperature, humidity, wind_speed
FROM doc.sensor_data
WHERE ts >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '24 hours'
ORDER BY ts DESC
LIMIT 50;

Per-location aggregation:

SELECT location,
       AVG(temperature) AS avg_temp,
       MIN(temperature) AS min_temp,
       MAX(temperature) AS max_temp,
       COUNT(*) AS samples
FROM doc.sensor_data
GROUP BY location
ORDER BY avg_temp DESC;

Hourly rollup:

SELECT date_trunc('hour', ts) AS hour_bucket,
       location,
       AVG(temperature) AS avg_temp,
       AVG(humidity) AS avg_humidity
FROM doc.sensor_data
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY 1, 2;

docs.title: Operational checks
docs.text:
SELECT table_name, number_of_shards, number_of_replicas
FROM information_schema.tables
WHERE table_schema = 'doc' AND table_name = 'sensor_data';

SHOW CREATE TABLE doc.sensor_data;

docs.title: Vector Workloads
docs.text: Use FLOAT_VECTOR columns for semantic retrieval and nearest-neighbor search.

docs.title: Create table
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS doc.documents (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  content TEXT,
  embedding FLOAT_VECTOR(4) WITH (similarity = 'cosine')
);

docs.title: Upsert sample vectors
docs.text:
INSERT INTO doc.documents (id, content, embedding)
VALUES
  ('doc_1', 'MonkDB is great for time-series and vector workloads.', [0.91,0.07,0.01,0.22]),
  ('doc_2', 'Vector search in databases is important for AI applications.', [0.11,0.80,0.05,0.33]),
  ('doc_3', 'MonkDB provides scalable distributed storage.', [0.84,0.10,0.02,0.18])
ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE
SET content = excluded.content,
    embedding = excluded.embedding;

REFRESH TABLE doc.documents;

docs.title: KNN query
docs.text:
WITH q AS (SELECT [0.10,0.79,0.06,0.30]::float_vector(4) AS v)
SELECT id, content, _score
FROM doc.documents
WHERE KNN_MATCH(embedding, (SELECT v FROM q), 2)
ORDER BY _score DESC;

docs.title: Similarity scoring query
docs.text:
WITH q AS (SELECT [0.10,0.79,0.06,0.30]::float_vector(4) AS v)
SELECT id,
       VECTOR_SIMILARITY(embedding, (SELECT v FROM q)) AS score
FROM doc.documents
ORDER BY score DESC
LIMIT 3;

docs.title: Similarity override (must match column similarity)
docs.text:
WITH q AS (SELECT [0.10,0.79,0.06,0.30]::float_vector(4) AS v)
SELECT id,
       VECTOR_SIMILARITY(embedding, (SELECT v FROM q), 'cosine') AS score
FROM doc.documents
WHERE KNN_MATCH(embedding, (SELECT v FROM q), 2, 'cosine')
ORDER BY score DESC;

docs.title: Full-Text Search Workloads
docs.text: Use full-text indexes and MATCH() for relevance-ranked text retrieval.

docs.title: Create table with FULLTEXT index
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS doc.articles (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  title TEXT,
  content TEXT,
  INDEX content_ft USING FULLTEXT (content) WITH (analyzer = 'english')
);

docs.title: Insert sample rows
docs.text:
INSERT INTO doc.articles (id, title, content) VALUES
  (1, 'Machine Learning', 'Machine learning algorithms power modern AI applications.'),
  (2, 'AI Ethics', 'Ethical considerations in AI are crucial for fairness and bias mitigation.'),
  (3, 'Databases', 'Distributed SQL systems support operational and analytical workloads.');

REFRESH TABLE doc.articles;

docs.title: MATCH query with relevance
docs.text:
SELECT id, title, content, _score
FROM doc.articles
WHERE MATCH(content, 'AI')
ORDER BY _score DESC;

docs.title: Multi-column weighted search
docs.text:
SELECT id, title, content, _score
FROM doc.articles
WHERE MATCH((title 2.0, content), 'machine learning')
ORDER BY _score DESC;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• LIKE is not a substitute for full-text ranking semantics.
  • Keep analyzers aligned to language and tokenization needs.

docs.title: Geospatial Workloads
docs.text: MonkDB supports geo_point and geo_shape for location search and spatial filtering.

docs.title: Create tables
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS doc.geo_points (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  location GEO_POINT
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS doc.geo_shapes (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  region GEO_SHAPE
);

docs.title: Insert sample data
docs.text:
INSERT INTO doc.geo_points (id, location) VALUES
  (1, [5.0, 5.0]),
  (2, [-8.0, -8.0]),
  (3, [15.0, 15.0]),
  (4, [-12.0, 5.0]);

INSERT INTO doc.geo_shapes (id, region) VALUES
  (1, 'POLYGON ((-10 -10, 10 -10, 10 10, -10 10, -10 -10))');

REFRESH TABLE doc.geo_points, doc.geo_shapes;

docs.title: Find points within polygon
docs.text:
SELECT p.id, p.location
FROM doc.geo_points p,
     doc.geo_shapes s
WHERE s.id = 1
  AND within(p.location, s.region)
ORDER BY p.id;

docs.title: Distance sort from a reference point
docs.text:
SELECT id,
       distance(location, [0.0, 0.0]) AS dist_m
FROM doc.geo_points
ORDER BY dist_m ASC;

docs.title: 3D + WKB context
docs.text: For WKB and Z-value retention behavior, see Geospatial WKB and 3D Retention.

docs.title: Document / JSON Workloads
docs.text: MonkDB supports JSON-like storage via OBJECT and collection handling via ARRAY.

docs.title: Create tables
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS doc.users (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT,
  address OBJECT(DYNAMIC),
  roles ARRAY(TEXT)
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS doc.articles (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  title TEXT,
  tags ARRAY(TEXT)
);

docs.title: Insert samples
docs.text:
INSERT INTO doc.users (id, name, address, roles) VALUES
  (1, 'Alice', {city='New York', zipcode='10001'}, ['admin','editor']),
  (2, 'Bob',   {city='London', zipcode='SW1A'},   ['viewer']);

INSERT INTO doc.articles (id, title, tags) VALUES
  (1, 'Intro to MonkDB', ['database','sql','scalability']);

REFRESH TABLE doc.users, doc.articles;

docs.title: Query nested fields and arrays
docs.text:
SELECT id, name, address['city'] AS city
FROM doc.users
WHERE address['zipcode'] = '10001';

SELECT *
FROM doc.users
WHERE 'admin' = ANY(roles);

SELECT id, array_length(tags) AS tag_count
FROM doc.articles
WHERE array_length(tags) >= 3;

docs.title: Useful scalar helpers
docs.text:
SELECT object_keys(address), object_values(address) FROM doc.users;
SELECT array_length(tags) FROM doc.articles;

docs.title: Related function references
docs.text:
• Scalar Functions Catalog
  • SQL Value Expressions

docs.title: BLOB Workloads
docs.text: Use BLOB tables for binary payload storage and retrieval via HTTP endpoints.

docs.title: Create BLOB table
docs.text:
CREATE BLOB TABLE media
CLUSTERED INTO 3 SHARDS;

docs.title: Upload / download / delete
docs.text:
MonkDB BLOBs are addressed by SHA-1 hash of content.

# upload
curl -X PUT "http://localhost:6000/_blobs/media/<SHA1>" --data-binary @sample.pdf

# download
curl -X GET "http://localhost:6000/_blobs/media/<SHA1>" -o downloaded.pdf

# delete
curl -X DELETE "http://localhost:6000/_blobs/media/<SHA1>"

docs.title: Production pattern: metadata table
docs.text:
BLOB tables do not provide rich SQL metadata browsing by default. Keep a relational metadata table:

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS doc.blob_metadata (
  file_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  blob_sha1 TEXT,
  logical_path TEXT,
  content_type TEXT,
  uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

This enables list/filter/audit queries without scanning binary payload storage.

docs.title: Operational caveats
docs.text:
• Treat BLOB endpoint auth and TLS as mandatory in non-local environments.
  • Use snapshot/backup strategy appropriate to your BLOB durability requirements.
  • Prefer external object store integration when advanced object lifecycle/versioning is required.

docs.title: Graph Workloads
docs.text: This guide demonstrates graph modeling and traversal using SQL graph objects.

docs.title: 1) Create graph and base tables
docs.text:
CREATE GRAPH social;

CREATE TABLE doc.users (
  id TEXT NOT NULL,
  name TEXT,
  segment TEXT,
  embedding FLOAT_VECTOR(4),
  event_ts TIMESTAMPTZ,
  metric DOUBLE PRECISION,
  PRIMARY KEY (id)
);

CREATE TABLE doc.follows (
  src_id INT NOT NULL,
  dst_id INT NOT NULL,
  event_ts TIMESTAMPTZ,
  weight DOUBLE PRECISION,
  PRIMARY KEY (src_id, dst_id)
);

CREATE TABLE doc.likes (
  src_id INT NOT NULL,
  dst_id INT NOT NULL,
  event_ts TIMESTAMPTZ,
  weight DOUBLE PRECISION,
  PRIMARY KEY (src_id, dst_id)
);

docs.title: 2) Register vertex and edge tables
docs.text:
CREATE VERTEX TABLE doc.users FOR GRAPH social KEY id;
CREATE EDGE TABLE doc.follows FOR GRAPH social SOURCE KEY src_id TARGET KEY dst_id;
CREATE EDGE TABLE doc.likes   FOR GRAPH social SOURCE KEY src_id TARGET KEY dst_id;

docs.title: 3) Insert sample data
docs.text:
INSERT INTO doc.users (id, name, segment, embedding, event_ts, metric) VALUES
  (1, 'Alice', 'creator', CAST([0.91,0.07,0.01,0.22] AS FLOAT_VECTOR(4)), '2026-02-23T10:00:00Z', 12.4),
  (2, 'Bob',   'viewer',  CAST([0.11,0.80,0.05,0.33] AS FLOAT_VECTOR(4)), '2026-02-23T10:05:00Z',  8.1),
  (3, 'Cara',  'creator', CAST([0.84,0.10,0.02,0.18] AS FLOAT_VECTOR(4)), '2026-02-23T10:10:00Z', 15.9),
  (4, 'Dinesh','viewer',  CAST([0.20,0.71,0.04,0.40] AS FLOAT_VECTOR(4)), '2026-02-23T10:15:00Z',  7.7);

INSERT INTO doc.follows (src_id, dst_id, event_ts, weight) VALUES
  (1,2,'2026-02-23T11:00:00Z',0.9),
  (2,3,'2026-02-23T11:01:00Z',0.8);

INSERT INTO doc.likes (src_id, dst_id, event_ts, weight) VALUES
  (1,3,'2026-02-23T12:00:00Z',0.5),
  (3,1,'2026-02-23T12:01:00Z',0.4);

REFRESH TABLE doc.users, doc.follows, doc.likes;

docs.title: 4) Discover graph metadata
docs.text:
SELECT * FROM graph.graphs;
SELECT * FROM graph.vertices;
SELECT * FROM graph.edges;

docs.title: 5) Graph queries
docs.text:
SELECT *
FROM graph_edges('social')
ORDER BY table_name, source_vertex, target_vertex;

SELECT *
FROM graph_neighbors('social', '1')
ORDER BY neighbor_vertex;

SELECT vertex, depth, path
FROM traverse('social','1',2)
ORDER BY depth, vertex;

docs.title: 6) Hybrid graph + analytics query
docs.text:
SELECT date_trunc('hour', event_ts) AS hr, count(*)
FROM doc.follows
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;

docs.title: 7) Hybrid graph + vector query
docs.text:
SELECT id, name
FROM doc.users
ORDER BY vector_similarity(
  embedding,
  [0.90, 0.10, 0.00, 0.20]::float_vector(4)
) DESC
LIMIT 3;

docs.title: Memory Workloads
docs.text: This guide demonstrates operational usage of MonkDB memory objects for session/context storage patterns.

docs.title: 1) Create base incident table
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE incidents (
  incident_id TEXT,
  summary TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP
);

INSERT INTO incidents (incident_id, summary, created_at) VALUES
  ('inc-1', 'payment failure in eu', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP),
  ('inc-2', 'card auth timeout', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);

docs.title: 2) Create memory object
docs.text:
CREATE MEMORY session_store
WITH (strict = true, entry_ttl_ms = 3600000, max_entries = 1000);

docs.title: 3) Inspect memory metadata
docs.text:
SELECT memory_name, options['strict'], options['entry_ttl_ms'], options['max_entries']
FROM memory.memories
WHERE memory_name = 'session_store';

docs.title: 4) Insert key-value entries
docs.text:
INSERT INTO memory.memory_entries (memory_name, entry_key, entry_value)
VALUES
  ('session_store', 'sess-42:intent', 'payment_failure'),
  ('session_store', 'sess-42:priority', 'urgent');

docs.title: 5) Read entries
docs.text:
SELECT memory_name, entry_key, entry_value
FROM memory.memory_entries
WHERE memory_name = 'session_store'
ORDER BY entry_key;

docs.title: 6) Upsert with conflict handling
docs.text:
INSERT INTO memory.memory_entries (memory_name, entry_key, entry_value)
VALUES ('session_store', 'sess-42:intent', 'payment_failure_v2')
ON CONFLICT (memory_name, entry_key) DO UPDATE
SET entry_value = excluded.entry_value;

docs.title: 7) Compact and monitor
docs.text:
ALTER MEMORY session_store COMPACT;

SELECT last_compacted_at IS NOT NULL,
       last_compaction_pruned_entries,
       total_compaction_pruned_entries
FROM memory.memories
WHERE memory_name = 'session_store';

SELECT strict, entry_ttl_ms, max_entries, active_entries, expired_entries, capacity_used_percent
FROM memory.memory_status
WHERE memory_name = 'session_store';

docs.title: 8) Cleanup
docs.text: DROP MEMORY session_store;

docs.title: Governance, Audit, Lineage, Contracts, and Policies Workloads
docs.text: This workload guide focuses on policy enforcement and governance controls with end-to-end examples.

docs.title: 1) Row filter policy workload
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE doc.users_rf (id INT PRIMARY KEY, tenant_id INT, email STRING) WITH (number_of_replicas = 0);
INSERT INTO doc.users_rf VALUES (1, 42, 'a@acme.com'), (2, 7, 'b@other.com');

CREATE POLICY users_rf_policy
FOR TABLE doc.users_rf
WITH (scope = 'row_filter', principal = '*', predicate = 'tenant_id = 42', precedence = 100);

GRANT DQL ON TABLE doc.users_rf TO testuser;

As testuser:

SET SESSION governance.enabled = true;
SET SESSION audit.enabled = true;
SELECT id, tenant_id, email FROM doc.users_rf ORDER BY id;

docs.title: 2) Column masking workload
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE doc.users_mask (id INT PRIMARY KEY, email STRING) WITH (number_of_replicas = 0);
INSERT INTO doc.users_mask VALUES (1, 'alpha@company.com'), (2, 'beta@company.com');

CREATE POLICY users_email_mask_policy
FOR TABLE doc.users_mask COLUMN email
WITH (scope = 'column_mask', principal = '*', masking_expression = '''***''', precedence = 100);

GRANT DQL ON TABLE doc.users_mask TO testuser;

As testuser:

SET SESSION governance.enabled = true;
SET SESSION audit.enabled = true;
SELECT id, email FROM doc.users_mask ORDER BY id;

docs.title: 3) Contract validation workload
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE doc.users_contract (id INT PRIMARY KEY, email STRING) WITH (number_of_replicas = 0);
INSERT INTO doc.users_contract VALUES (1, 'ok@x.com'), (2, 'invalid_email');

CREATE CONTRACT users_email_format
FOR TABLE doc.users_contract COLUMN email
WITH (version = 1, severity = 'high', expression = 'email like ''%@%''', mode = 'warn');

VALIDATE TABLE doc.users_contract;

SELECT run_id, table_name, status, checked_contracts, violation_count
FROM governance.validation_runs
WHERE table_name = 'doc.users_contract'
ORDER BY started_at DESC
LIMIT 5;

docs.title: 4) AI usage policy workload
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE doc.ai_users (id INT PRIMARY KEY, email STRING) WITH (number_of_replicas = 0);
INSERT INTO doc.ai_users VALUES (1, 'a@x');
REFRESH TABLE doc.ai_users;

CREATE CONTRACT ai_users_enforce_contract
FOR TABLE doc.ai_users COLUMN email
WITH (version = 1, severity = 'high', expression = 'email like ''%@%''', mode = 'warn');

CREATE POLICY ai_usage_enforce_policy
FOR TABLE doc.ai_users
WITH (
  scope = 'ai_usage',
  principal = '*',
  allowed_purposes = 'fraud_scoring',
  allowed_models = 'risk-model-v1',
  max_contract_severity = 'medium',
  mode = 'enforce',
  precedence = 100
);

As testuser:

SET SESSION governance.enabled = true;

SET SESSION purpose = 'batch_reporting';
SET SESSION model_id = 'risk-model-v2';
SELECT id FROM doc.ai_users;

SET SESSION purpose = 'fraud_scoring';
SET SESSION model_id = 'risk-model-v1';
SELECT id FROM doc.ai_users;

docs.title: 5) Lineage workload
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE doc.l_src (id INT PRIMARY KEY) WITH (number_of_replicas = 0);
CREATE TABLE doc.l_dst (id INT PRIMARY KEY) WITH (number_of_replicas = 0);

GRANT DQL, DML ON TABLE doc.l_src TO testuser;
GRANT DQL, DML ON TABLE doc.l_dst TO testuser;

As testuser:

SET SESSION lineage.enabled = true;
SET SESSION pipeline_id = 'pipeline-ctx';
SET SESSION model_id = 'model-ctx';
SET SESSION dataset_version = 'v-ctx';
SET SESSION purpose = 'fraud_scoring';
SET SESSION trace_id = 'trace-ctx';

INSERT INTO doc.l_src (id) VALUES (1), (2);
INSERT INTO doc.l_dst (id) SELECT id FROM doc.l_src;

As superuser:

SELECT source_table, target_table, transform_metadata['operation']
FROM sys.lineage_edges
WHERE source_table = 'doc.l_src' AND target_table = 'doc.l_dst'
ORDER BY timestamp DESC
LIMIT 5;
